content: When you enable CONFIG_RUST, this also implicitly enables CONFIG_CONSTRUCTORS due to a select in the Kconfig. Why do we have this?

See commit 4b9a68f2e59a0 ("rust: add support for static synchronisation primitives") in the old rust branch:

    rust: add support for static synchronisation primitives

    This allows drivers to declare global shared variables (`static`) that
    can be mutated when the synchronisation primitive is held.

    Although we want to discourage the use of globals, some drivers still
    need them. For example, a driver that needs to allocate an id to each
    instance of a device that is attached (and free it up when it's
    detached) may have a global variable to manage ids, access to which
    needs to synchronised.

    We use constructors to implement this, so we select
    `CONFIG_CONSTRUCTORS` automatically when `RUST` is selected.

i.e. init_static_sync! used .ctors (later .init_array): https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/rust/kernel/sync.rs#L135

@Wedson Almeida Filho
Okay, but we don't actually support that right now.
Perhaps it would make more sense to remove the select and gate such features behind #[cfg(CONFIG_CONSTRUCTORS)]?

I mean, in general, I do not think that CONFIG_CONSTRUCTORS is the right choice for global mutexes. We can replicate the C macro for global mutexes, and code-before-main is in general fraught with issues. For example, it looks like the init_static_sync! macro is unsound because one global could access another global during initialization, which will lead to issues depending on the order in which they are initialized.
Unless I hear any objections, I'm going to submit a patch that removes select CONSTRUCTORS.

No objections from me. if we still need something like this in the future, we can discuss then what to do.

@Benno Lossin , I think we briefly discussed this in some forum but I don't remember which anymore, so restarting the discussion here. How can we use pin_init to initialise static instances of pinned structures like locks?
the only thing we need to do would be to first construct a impl PinInit value and then call PinInit::__pinned_init() from the initializer code.
I think that we should not be using initializers unless it is absolutely necessary. It is not necessary for locks; there's nothing preventing us from replicating the DEFINE_SPINLOCK macro in Rust.
of course we can just have a macro that wraps that, I think @Wedson Almeida Filho was after how the internals would work
If you replicate the DEFINE_SPINLOCK macro to avoid needing initializers, then you probably can't use pin-init since traits are not const.

the question is what are the use cases for statics? I personally do not use them at all, but in userspace that might be easier. If they can be avoided most of the time, then having a solution that only allows simple use-cases might be better than a general mechanism to initialize statics via pin-init. But if people want to e.g. initialize structures with nested locks etc. then we should probably use pin-init.
My only use of globals is a mutex containing a linked list of all binder contexts.
